project.title: Dendritic cells infected with Salmonella enterica
project.projectDescription: Salmonella enterica causes serious global burden of morbidity and mortality and is a major cause of infant bacteremia in sub Saharan Africa. Diseases caused by Salmonella are treatable with antibiotics but successful antibiotic treatment has become difficult due to antimicrobial resistance. An effective vaccine together with public health effort may therefore be a better strategy to control these infections. Protective immunity against Salmonella depends primarily on T cell-mediated immune responses and therefore identifying relevant T cell antigens is necessary for Salmonella vaccine development. Our laboratory has used an immunoproteomics approach to identify Chlamydia T cell antigens that exhibited significant protection against Chlamydia infection in mice. In this study, we infected murine bone marrow derived dendritic cells from C57BL/6 mice with Salmonella enterica strain SL1344 followed by isolation of MHC class I and II- molecules and elution of bound peptides. The sequences of the peptides were then identified using tandem mass spectrometry. We identified 87 MHC class II and 23 MHC class I Salmonella derived peptides. Four of 12 peptides stimulated IFN-? production by CD4 T cells from the spleens of mice with persistent Salmonella infection. These antigens will be useful for Salmonella immunobiology research and are potential Salmonella vaccine candidates.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Immunoenriched MHC molecules followed by elution of MHC-bound peptides. Peptides analyzed by LC-MS/MS.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Mascot search without enzyme specificity against mouse uniprot database.

references.referenceLine: Karunakaran KP, Yu H, Jiang X, Chan Q, Goldberg MF, Jenkins MK, Foster LJ, Brunham RC. Identification of MHC-bound peptides from dendritic cells infected with Salmonella enterica strain SLimplications for a non-typhoidal Salmonella vaccine. J Proteome Res. 2016 Nov 1

Characteristics[Treatment]: BMDCs were infected with Salmonella for 0.5 h at 2.5 MOI and were incubated for an additional 14 h.
Comment[EnrichmentMethod]: the MHC-bound peptides were isolated using MHC allele-specific monoclonal antibody affinity columns
eluted from rPAS by adding 4 bed volumes of 0.2 N acetic acid, and 1/10th volume of 1.6 N acetic acid was then added to the elute to further separate the peptides from MHC molecules.
Comment[FractionationMethod]: separated from high-molecular-weight material using 10 kDa molecular weight cutoff filters.
Comment[Instrument]: LTQ-Orbitrap Velos (Thermo Electron) online coupled to Agilent 1200 Series nanoflow HPLCs using nanospray ionization sources (Proxeon Biosystems, Odense, Denmark).
Comment[IonizationType]: nanospray ionization sources (Proxeon Biosystems, Odense, Denmark)
Comment[AcquisitionMethod]: The Velos was set to acquire a full range scan at 60 000 resolution in the Orbitrap, from which the 10 most intense multiply charged ions per cycle were isolated for fragmentation in the LTQ.
Comment[GradientTime]: Gradients were run from 10% B to 32% B over 51 min, then from 32% B to 40% B in the next 5 min, then increased to 100% B over 2 min period, held at 100% B for 2.5 min, and then dropped to 0% B for another 20 min to recondition the column.
Comment[Separation]: Analytical columns were packed into 15 cm long, 50 μm inner diameter fused silica emitters using 3 μm diameter ReproSil Pur C18 AQ beads (Dr. Maisch, www.Dr-Maisch.com), joint with 2 cm long, 100 μm inner diameter fused silica trap column packed with 5 μm diameter Aqua C-18 beads (Phenomenex, www.phenomenex.com), and a 20 μm inner diameter fused silica gold-coated spray tip with 6 μm diameter opening.
